Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Introduction
A few months ago, I was working on a frontend project when I got stuck with a JavaScript error.
After trying different solutions for almost an hour, I opened ChatGPT and typed:
“Can you help me fix this JavaScript error?”
Within seconds, it explained the problem, suggested a fix, and even told me why the error was happening.
That was impressive.
Then I started using ChatGPT for other things — writing emails, learning React Native, summarizing documentation, and even understanding AI concepts.
But one question kept coming back to me.
How does ChatGPT actually understand my questions?
Does it search Google every time?
Does it already know everything?
Or is there something happening behind the scenes that most people don’t know about?
That curiosity led me to learn about Large Language Models (LLMs), and in this article, I’ll explain them in simple English.
If you’re a student, developer, or just curious about AI, this article is for you.

What is an LLM?
LLM stands for Large Language Model.
In simple words, it’s an AI model that’s trained on a huge amount of text from books, articles, websites, research papers, and other sources.
During training, it doesn’t memorize every sentence. Instead, it learns how language works.
It learns things like:

• Grammar

• Sentence structure

• Relationships between words

• Context

• Writing patterns
When you ask ChatGPT a question, it doesn’t “look up” the answer like Google.
Instead, it predicts what the next most likely token should be based on everything you’ve typed.
That’s how it generates responses that sound natural.

What Problems Do LLMs Solve?
One thing I really like about LLMs is that they’re useful for everyday tasks.
For example, I personally use ChatGPT for:

• Debugging my code

• Writing emails

• Understanding documentation

• Learning new technologies

• Brainstorming ideas
But that’s just the beginning.
LLMs can also help with:

• Translation

• Content writing

• Summarizing long articles

• Answering questions

• Resume writing

• Customer support
Instead of learning hundreds of commands, you simply explain what you want in plain English.
Some Popular LLMs
Today, many companies have their own language models.
Some of the most popular ones are:

• ChatGPT (OpenAI)

• Gemini (Google)

• Claude (Anthropic)

• Llama (Meta)

• Mistral

• DeepSeek
Although they come from different companies, they all work using similar ideas.

What Happens When You Send a Message to ChatGPT?
Let’s say you type this:
“Explain Artificial Intelligence in simple words.”
It looks simple.
But internally, a lot happens in just a few seconds.
The process looks something like this:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why Can’t Computers Understand Language?
This was one of the biggest surprises for me.
Computers don’t actually understand English.
Or Hindi.
Or any human language.
They only understand numbers.
That means before ChatGPT can process your question, your text has to be converted into numbers.
That’s where tokenization comes in.

What is Tokenization?
Tokenization is simply breaking text into smaller pieces called tokens.
For example,
Press enter or click to view image in full size

could become
Sometimes even a single word gets split into multiple tokens.
The exact split depends on the tokenizer being used.

Meet the Transformer
After tokenization, the tokens are passed to something called a Transformer.
This is the technology behind almost every modern LLM.
Instead of reading one word at a time, the Transformer looks at the entire sentence and figures out how the words are related.
That’s why ChatGPT usually understands context much better than older AI models.
So, Does ChatGPT Copy Answers From the Internet?
This is probably the biggest misconception.
The answer is No — not usually.
ChatGPT doesn’t search the internet every time you ask a question.
Instead, it generates responses by predicting the next token based on patterns it learned during training.
If web browsing is enabled, it can use live information, but that’s a separate capability.
Conclusion
When I first started using ChatGPT, I thought it simply searched the internet for answers. After learning about Large Language Models, I realized there’s much more happening behind the scenes.
From tokenization to Transformers and next-token prediction, every response is generated through a fascinating process that happens in just a few seconds.
I hope this article helped you understand the basics of how ChatGPT works in a simple and easy-to-follow way.
